patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: btrfs_log_dev_io_error() on all bio errors

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch aims to log all bio errors in btrfs, not just those explicitly listed (BLK_STS_IOERR and BLK_STS_TARGET). The author suggests that the current filtering is misleading and can lead to incorrect conclusions about system behavior. They provide an example of a situation where many failures are reported as BLK_STS_MEDIUM on nvme devices.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it acceptable. The author's concern about misleading error reporting is valid, and Qu agrees that logging all bio errors is a good idea.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/3/14 05:56, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> Signed-off-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu


> ---
>   fs/btrfs/bio.c | 3 +--
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 2 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/bio.c b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> index 2a2a21aec817..08b1d62603d0 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/bio.c
> @@ -352,8 +352,7 @@ static void btrfs_log_dev_io_error(const struct bio *bio, struct btrfs_device *d
>   {
>   	if (!dev || !dev->bdev)
>   		return;
> -	if (bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_IOERR && bio->bi_status != BLK_STS_TARGET)
> -		return;
> +	ASSERT(bio->bi_status);
>   
>   	if (btrfs_op(bio) == BTRFS_MAP_WRITE)
>   		btrfs_dev_stat_inc_and_print(dev, BTRFS_DEV_STAT_WRITE_ERRS);

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns about the complexity of block error values and suggested leaving things as-is for now. He plans to address this issue in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> 
> For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().

This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
be very soon.

review_comments.author: Boris Burkov
review_comments.summary: Responded to Christoph's concerns, arguing that logging all bio errors is better than the current filtering. He also offered to help with addressing the block error values issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 05:20:20PM +0100, Christoph Hellwig wrote:
> On Fri, Mar 13, 2026 at 12:26:05PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> > As far as I can tell, we never intentionally constrained ourselves to
> > these status codes, and it is misleading and surprising to lack the
> > bdev error logging when we get a different error code from the block
> > layer. This can lead to jumping to a wrong conclusion like "this
> > system didn't see any bio failures but aborted with EIO".
> > 
> > For example on nvme devices, I observe many failures coming back as
> > BLK_STS_MEDIUM. It is apparent that the nvme driver returns a variety of
> > BLK_STS_* status values in nvme_error_status().
> 
> This goes all the way back to adding the error counts in commit
> 442a4f6308e69.  The block error values are a bit of a mess and it's
> on my todo list to sort them out - only a small number of them should
> even be seen here.  I'd suggest to leave things as-is for now, and
> I'll make sure you're included in the discussion when I get around
> to attack the block status vs file system issues, which will hopefully
> be very soon.
> 

Is there a reason not to properly log the errors we are definitely
already seeing? I have thousands of instances with swallowed
BLK_STS_MEDIUM errors that really confused me and messed up my data
while investigating a possible regression.

If you think this will confuse/upset users who aren't watching a giant
fleet with distributed logging and make them throw out good drives
prematurely or something, then I can just carry this patch internally.

If you are just worried about having to aim at a moving target, I am
happy to offer to be available to make sure btrfs does what you want
when you do find time to work on this. It's hard for me to judge since I
don't really know what is wrong with the error signalling as-is.

Put another way, if we are in a situation with technical debt, which is
what I think you are saying, then I would rather have more visibility
rather than less.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.summary: Suggested giving him a week to address the issue, and in the meantime, adding BLK_STS_MEDIUM to the whitelist.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] EVM: add comment describing why ino field is still unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the EVM code explaining why the inode number field 'ino' remains an unsigned long, despite the inode->i_ino being widened to a u64. The reason is to allow existing HMAC and signatures from 32-bit hosts to continue working when the inode number fits within a 32-bit value.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] nilfs2: fix 64-bit division operations in nilfs_bmap_find_target_in_group()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a build failure on 32-bit ARM caused by the introduction of u64 type for inode->i_ino, which led to undefined '__aeabi_uldivmod' symbol. The fix involves using the udiv_u64() function for division and replacing a multiplication with a bitwise AND operation to find the index into the group. Additionally, a compile-time assertion is added to ensure that NILFS_BMAP_GROUP_DIV is a power of two.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses follow-on issues related to the inode->i_ino widening series, specifically fixing a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page() and updating nilfs2 code for 64-bit division operations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1] iomap: fix invalid folio access when i_blkbits differs from I/O granularity

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1] iomap: fix invalid folio access when i_blkbits differs from I/O granularity

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue where the iomap_read_folio_iter function fails to invalidate ctx->cur_folio when the IO granularity differs from the block size, leading to potential double-put bugs. The fix involves unconditionally invalidating ctx->cur_folio in the !ifs case, ensuring that the IO helper owns and completes the read on the folio.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [BUG] fuse: wb_wait_for_completion hang on suspend with fuse-overlayfs on tmpfs persists in 6.19.6 (AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY fix incomplete)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: A bug report on fuse-overlayfs causing a hang on suspend due to the fuse daemon being stuck and unable to service write_inode() requests.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Identified the root cause of the hang as the fuse daemon being stuck and unable to service write_inode() requests. Provided a reproducible test case using a synthetic I/O workload.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Mar 17, 2026 at 4:25 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Mon, Mar 16, 2026 at 5:15 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > On Sun, Mar 15, 2026 at 4:24 AM John <therealgraysky@proton.me> wrote:
> > >
> >
> > Hi John,
> >
> > Thanks for your detailed report.
> >
> > > Kernel: 6.19.6-arch1-1
> > > Component: fs/fuse, fs/fs-writeback
> > >
> > > --- SUMMARY ---
> > >
> > > A suspend-to-RAM hang in wb_wait_for_completion() via sync_inodes_sb() persists on 6.19.6 when fuse-overlayfs is mounted on tmpfs. The fix introduced in 6.19~rc6 for Debian bug #1120058 ("fs/writeback: skip AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY mappings in wait_sb_inodes()") does not cover this code path.
> > >
> > > --- BACKGROUND ---
> > >
> > > This issue was originally reported in:
> > > https://github.com/containers/fuse-overlayfs/issues/386
> > >
> > > The fuse-overlayfs developer identified it as a kernel issue. It was subsequently bisected to commit 0c58a97f919c ("fuse: remove tmp folio for writebacks and internal rb tree", merged in 6.16) and tracked in Debian as bug #1120058. The fix applied in 6.19~rc6 targets wait_sb_inodes(), but the hang in the reporter's original trace and in this report occurs in sync_inodes_sb() via a separate code path that the fix does not reach.
> > >
> > > The issue is also known to syzbot as "INFO: task hung in sync_inodes_sb" with open instances on linux-5.15, linux-6.1, and linux-6.6:
> > >
> > > https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=e0232bd63c6e293aaf6a
> > > https://syzkaller.appspot.com/bug?extid=4983a35cf671e5ed55b3
> > >
> > > --- REPRODUCTION ---
> > >
> > > mkdir -p /dev/shm/test/up
> > > mkdir -p /dev/shm/test/tmp
> > > mkdir -p /dev/shm/test/data
> > >
> > > fuse-overlayfs -o "static_nlink,noacl,\
> > >   upperdir=/dev/shm/test/up,\
> > >   lowerdir=$HOME/.config/mozilla/firefox,\
> > >   workdir=/dev/shm/test/tmp" \
> > >   /dev/shm/test/data
> > >
> > > firefox --profile /dev/shm/test/data/PROFILENAME
> > >
> > > Browse to youtube and start playing a video then trigger suspend while the video is playing in the browser. I used XFCE4 suspend from the menu.
> > >
> > > The display goes blank and does not recover. The system does not enter suspend. Switching to a tty shows the system is alive but X11 is frozen. Reboot is blocked:
> > >
> > > # reboot
> > > Call to Reboot failed: Action suspend already in progress, refusing requested reboot operation.
> > >
> > > --- CALL TRACE (6.19.6) ---
> > >
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel: INFO: task kworker/u128:0:106160 blocked for more than 122 seconds.
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:       Not tainted 6.19.6-arch1-1 #1
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel: task:kworker/u128:0  state:D stack:0     pid:106160 tgid:106160 ppid:2
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel: Workqueue: pm_fs_sync pm_fs_sync_work_fn
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel: Call Trace:
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  <TASK>
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  __schedule+0x457/0x1720
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  schedule+0x27/0xd0
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  wb_wait_for_completion+0x97/0xe0
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  sync_inodes_sb+0xf8/0x2e0
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  __iterate_supers+0xdc/0x160
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  ksys_sync+0x43/0xb0
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  pm_fs_sync_work_fn+0x17/0xa0
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  process_one_work+0x193/0x350
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  worker_thread+0x1a1/0x310
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  kthread+0xfc/0x240
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  ret_from_fork+0x243/0x280
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  ret_from_fork_asm+0x1a/0x30
> > > Mar 15 06:44:42 kernel:  </TASK>
> > >
> > > --- MORE CONTEXT ---
> > >
> > > Compared to the original report (which ran through systemd-sleep -> pm_suspend -> ksys_sync), the sync call in 6.19 has been moved into a kernel workqueue via pm_fs_sync_work_fn. The hang point is identical: wb_wait_for_completion() inside sync_inodes_sb() never returns because the FUSE daemon (fuse-overlayfs) is unable to complete writeback while the suspend freezer is in progress.
> > >
> > > The AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY fix targets wait_sb_inodes(), which is a separate function from sync_inodes_sb(). The hung writeback completion wait in sync_inodes_sb() is not gated by the AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY check and remains unaddressed.
> >
> > I'll need to run the repro and verify this is the issue, but I think
> > it's because it's hitting this call chain if there's a dirty folio
> > that is already under writeback that needs to have writeback issued on
> > it again:
> >
> > wb_workfn()
> >   wb_do_writeback()
> >     wb_writeback()
> >       writeback_sb_inodes()
> >         __writeback_single_inode()
> >           do_writepages()
> >             fuse_writepages()
> >               iomap_writepages()
> >                 writeback_iter()
> >                   writeback_get_folio()
> >                     folio_prepare_writeback()
> >
> > where in the folio_prepare_writeback() logic:
> >
> >         if (!folio_test_dirty(folio))
> >                 return false;
> >
> >         if (folio_test_writeback(folio)) {
> >                 if (wbc->sync_mode == WB_SYNC_NONE)
> >                         return false;
> >                 folio_wait_writeback(folio);

Re: [BUG] fuse: wb_wait_for_completion hang on suspend with fuse-overlayfs on tmpfs persists in 6.19.6 (AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY fix incomplete)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes a hang issue in the Linux kernel's fuse module, specifically when using fuse-overlayfs on tmpfs and suspending the system. The problem occurs because the wb_wait_for_completion function is waiting for writeback to complete on an inode that has already been marked as dirty and under writeback. To fix this, the patch adds a check to skip inodes with no data integrity guarantees (e.g., FUSE mappings) when syncing. This approach ensures that the system does not hang indefinitely while waiting for writeback to complete on such inodes.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author Joanne Koong addressed John's concern about the wb_wait_for_completion hang on suspend with fuse-overlayfs on tmpfs by proposing a cleaner fix: adding a superblock flag SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY to indicate filesystems that cannot guarantee data persistence on sync, and skipping sync(2) inode writeback for such superblocks. This replaces the per-inode AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY mapping flag added in commit f9a49aa302a0.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think this is the cleaner fix:

writeback: skip sync(2) inode writeback for filesystems with no data
integrity guarantees

Add SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY superblock flag for filesystems that cannot
guarantee data persistence on sync (eg fuse) and skip sync(2) inode
writeback for superblocks with this flag set.

This replaces the per-inode AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY mapping flag added in
commit f9a49aa302a0 ("fs/writeback: skip AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY mappings
in wait_sb_inodes()"). The flag belongs at the superblock level because
data integrity is a filesystem-wide property,  not a per-inode one. This
also allows sync_inodes_one_sb() to skip the entire filesystem
efficiently, rather than iterating every dirty inode only to skip each
one individually.

Without this, sync(2) triggers writeback on FUSE inodes and may block
waiting for the daemon to complete issued writeback or setattr (from
->write_inode()) requests.

This restores fuse to its prior behavior before tmp folios were removed,
where sync was essentially a no-op.

Reported-by: John <therealgraysky@proton.me>
Fixes: 0c58a97f919c ("fuse: remove tmp folio for writebacks and
internal rb tree")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
---
 fs/fs-writeback.c              |  7 +------
 fs/fuse/file.c                 |  4 +---
 fs/fuse/inode.c                |  1 +
 fs/sync.c                      |  2 +-
 include/linux/fs/super_types.h |  1 +
 include/linux/pagemap.h        | 11 -----------
 6 files changed, 5 insertions(+), 21 deletions(-)

diff --git a/fs/fs-writeback.c b/fs/fs-writeback.c
index 8f8069fb76ba..7a02483e0d8d 100644
--- a/fs/fs-writeback.c
+++ b/fs/fs-writeback.c
@@ -2774,13 +2774,8 @@ static void wait_sb_inodes(struct super_block *sb)
                 * The mapping can appear untagged while still on-list since we
                 * do not have the mapping lock. Skip it here, wb completion
                 * will remove it.
-                *
-                * If the mapping does not have data integrity semantics,
-                * there's no need to wait for the writeout to complete, as the
-                * mapping cannot guarantee that data is persistently stored.
                 */
-               if (!mapping_tagged(mapping, PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK) ||
-                   mapping_no_data_integrity(mapping))
+               if (!mapping_tagged(mapping, PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK))
                        continue;

                spin_unlock_irq(&sb->s_inode_wblist_lock);
diff --git a/fs/fuse/file.c b/fs/fuse/file.c
index 7294bd347412..111ccc5bdda3 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/file.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/file.c
@@ -3205,10 +3205,8 @@ void fuse_init_file_inode(struct inode *inode,
unsigned int flags)

        inode->i_fop = &fuse_file_operations;
        inode->i_data.a_ops = &fuse_file_aops;
-       if (fc->writeback_cache) {
+       if (fc->writeback_cache)
                mapping_set_writeback_may_deadlock_on_reclaim(&inode->i_data);
-               mapping_set_no_data_integrity(&inode->i_data);
-       }

        INIT_LIST_HEAD(&fi->write_files);
        INIT_LIST_HEAD(&fi->queued_writes);
diff --git a/fs/fuse/inode.c b/fs/fuse/inode.c
index af8ad96829fd..bae7d9ac3a43 100644
--- a/fs/fuse/inode.c
+++ b/fs/fuse/inode.c
@@ -1769,6 +1769,7 @@ static void fuse_sb_defaults(struct super_block *sb)
        sb->s_export_op = &fuse_export_operations;
        sb->s_iflags |= SB_I_IMA_UNVERIFIABLE_SIGNATURE;
        sb->s_iflags |= SB_I_NOIDMAP;
+       sb->s_iflags |= SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY;
        if (sb->s_user_ns != &init_user_ns)
                sb->s_iflags |= SB_I_UNTRUSTED_MOUNTER;
        sb->s_flags &= ~(SB_NOSEC | SB_I_VERSION);
diff --git a/fs/sync.c b/fs/sync.c
index 942a60cfedfb..88c08e2f76b2 100644
--- a/fs/sync.c
+++ b/fs/sync.c
@@ -73,7 +73,7 @@ EXPORT_SYMBOL(sync_filesystem);

 static void sync_inodes_one_sb(struct super_block *sb, void *arg)
 {
-       if (!sb_rdonly(sb))
+       if (!sb_rdonly(sb) && !(sb->s_iflags & SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY))
                sync_inodes_sb(sb);
 }

diff --git a/include/linux/fs/super_types.h b/include/linux/fs/super_types.h
index fa7638b81246..383050e7fdf5 100644
--- a/include/linux/fs/super_types.h
+++ b/include/linux/fs/super_types.h
@@ -338,5 +338,6 @@ struct super_block {
 #define SB_I_NOUMASK   0x00001000      /* VFS does not apply umask */
 #define SB_I_NOIDMAP   0x00002000      /* No idmapped mounts on this
superblock */
 #define SB_I_ALLOW_HSM 0x00004000      /* Allow HSM events on this
superblock */
+#define SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY 0x00008000 /* fs cannot guarantee data
persistence on sync */

 #endif /* _LINUX_FS_SUPER_TYPES_H */
diff --git a/include/linux/pagemap.h b/include/linux/pagemap.h
index ec442af3f886..31a848485ad9 100644
--- a/include/linux/pagemap.h
+++ b/include/linux/pagemap.h
@@ -210,7 +210,6 @@ enum mapping_flags {
        AS_WRITEBACK_MAY_DEADLOCK_ON_RECLAIM = 9,
        AS_KERNEL_FILE = 10,    /* mapping for a fake kernel file that shouldn't
                                   account usage to user cgroups */
-       AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY = 11, /* no data integrity guarantees */
        /* Bits 16-25 are used for FOLIO_ORDER */
        AS_FOLIO_ORDER_BITS = 5,
        AS_FOLIO_ORDER_MIN = 16,
@@ -346,16 +345,6 @@ static inline bool
mapping_writeback_may_deadlock_on_reclaim(const struct addres
        return test_bit(AS_WRITEBACK_MAY_DEADLOCK_ON_RECLAIM, &mapping->flags);
 }

-static inline void mapping_set_no_data_integrity(struct address_space *mapping)
-{
-       set_bit(AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY, &mapping->flags);
-}
-
-static inline bool mapping_no_data_integrity(const struct
address_space *mapping)
-{
-       return test_bit(AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY, &mapping->flags);
-}
-
 static inline gfp_t mapping_gfp_mask(const struct address_space *mapping)
 {
        return mapping->gfp_mask;

I tested it locally on my simulated repro and it fixed the issue for
me. Could you verify that this patch fixes the issue for you as well?

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is an example use case where this feature is necessary to keep PG_partially_mapped and vmstats consistent. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru, improving code consistency and reducing complexity.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) suggested improving the lock_list_lru() function by adding a /*skip=empty=*/true flag and making the bool parameters more consistent, but noted that it's an internal function so not urgent.

Reviewer David pointed out a typo in the documentation of list_lru_lock_irqsave, suggesting that 'false' should be replaced with 'NULL'.

Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
The two "bool" parameters really are ugly. Fortunately this is only an
internal function.

The callers are still a bit hard to read; we could add /*skip=empty=*/true).

like

return lock_list_lru_of_memcg(lru, nid, memcg, /* irq= */false, NULL,
			      /* skip_empty= */false);

Like we do in other code. But I guess we should do it consistently then
(or better add some proper flags).

Anyhow, something that could be cleaned up later.

---

And here it gets really confusing. true false false ... am I reading
binary code?

I guess the second "false" should actually be "NULL" :)

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that the current locking is not ideal and expresses frustration about it, but does not commit to a specific fix or revision.

Author agrees that adding caller locking is a good idea and plans to include it in a future patch revision (v3) to fix the __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() function and add a lockdep key.
sentiment_signals: plans to address in future patch
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah, I absolutely hate this too. I only didn't look further because
it's internal, but...

---

This is a great idea.

I have to send a v3 for the fix in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped()
and the lockdep key, so I'll make this change along with it.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function, memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio(), which allocates list_lru heads for a given folio under memory cgroup (memcg) control. The function is designed to be lightweight and efficient, allowing it to operate under RCU protection without pinning the memcg. This change is part of a larger effort to switch the THP shrinker to use list_lru.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Suggested renaming the function to better reflect its purpose and behavior. Provided two alternative name suggestions: folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() or memcg_list_lru_alloc_for_folio().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the deferred split shrinker's queue handling with list_lru, which sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages across nodes. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special migration code.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) suggested combining vma_alloc_folio and memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio into a single API, vma_alloc_anon_folio, to simplify anon alloc paths and reduce code duplication.

Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the patch introduces a potential RCU lock ordering issue and suggested using get/put-like logic or helpers to simplify the list_lru_lock+unlock operations.

Reviewer noted that the patch looks mostly mechanical and is well done, but requires another review pass for thoroughness.
review_comments.raw_body:
So, in all anon alloc paths, we essentialy have

1) vma_alloc_folio / __folio_alloc (khugepaged being odd)
2) mem_cgroup_charge / mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio
3) memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio

I wonder if we could do better in most cases and have something like a

	vma_alloc_anon_folio()

That wraps the vma_alloc_folio() + memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio(), but
still leaves the charging to the caller?

The would at least combine 1) and 3) in a single API. (except for the
odd cases without a VMA).

I guess we would want to skip the memcg_list_lru_alloc_folio() for
order-0 folios, correct?

---

The RCU lock is for the folio_memcg(), right?

I recall I raised in the past that some get/put-like logic (that wraps
the rcu_read_lock() + folio_memcg()) might make this a lot easier to get.


memcg = folio_memcg_lookup(folio)

... do stuff

folio_memcg_putback(folio, memcg);

Or sth like that.


Alternativey, you could have some helpers that do the
list_lru_lock+unlock etc.

folio_memcg_list_lru_lock()
...
folio_memcg_list_ru_unlock(l);

Just some thoughts as inspiration :)

---

[...]

Most changes here look mostly mechanically, quite nice. I'll probably
have to go over some bits once again with a fresh mind :)

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the charging mechanism is responsible for setting folio_memcg(), which was a concern about how the deferred split queue handles cgroups, and did not commit to revising the patch.

Author acknowledged that order-1 folios are not added to the deferred split queue, explaining it's due to memory constraints and no significant waste.

Author acknowledged David's concern about inconsistent RCU locking patterns and agreed to address it in a future patch, but not in the current submission.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm, but it's the charging that figures out the memcg and sets
folio_memcg() :(

---

Yeah, we don't use the queue for < order-1. In deferred_split_folio():

	/*
	 * Order 1 folios have no space for a deferred list, but we also
	 * won't waste much memory by not adding them to the deferred list.
	 */
	if (folio_order(folio) <= 1)
		return;

---

I remember you raising this in the objcg + reparenting patches. There
are a few more instances of

	rcu_read_lock()
	foo = folio_memcg()
	...
	rcu_read_unlock()

in other parts of the code not touched by these patches here, so the
first pattern is a more universal encapsulation.

Let me look into this. Would you be okay with a follow-up that covers
the others as well?

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that implementing list_lru for deferred split shrinker requires additional complexity, as it needs to handle three operations simultaneously, and pointed out a discrepancy in collapse_file() which allocates anonymous folios without placing them on the queue.

Reviewer suggested that the list_lru lock helpers should be moved to a different series if they are indeed correct
sentiment_signals: questioning the correctness of the patch
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh ... right. I guess we would then have to do all 3 things at the same
time, which makes the helper a bit more involved.

I'll note that collapse_file() also calls alloc_charge_folio(), but not
for allocating an anonymous folio that would have to be placed on the
deferred split queue.

---

Of course :) If list_lru lock helpers would be the right thing to do, it
might be better placed in this series.

-- 
Cheers,

David

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use the list_lru data structure instead of its current implementation. The motivation for this change is to address scalability issues in the original implementation, which involved a complex sequence of function calls. The new approach uses the list_lru API to manage the LRU state and allows for more fine-grained control over locking. The patches also include various cleanups and refactors to make the code easier to review and maintain.

There is some overlap and conflict between your delta and what later
patches in the series do.

AFAICS, the main thing left over would be: to have
__lock_list_lru_of_memcg() for the reclaimer (which does not walk the
parents during a cgroup deletion race) and lock_list_lru_of_memcg()

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/11] mm/zswap, zsmalloc: Per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces per-memcg-lruvec zswap accounting by moving memory-cgroup awareness to the zsmalloc layer, allowing for more accurate tracking of compressed memory usage and enabling cgroups to control where their zswapped objects live. The changes also enable future consumers to account memory statistics using a new compressed object memory accounting infrastructure. The patch series includes 11 patches that make various modifications to the zswap and zsmalloc code to achieve this goal, including introducing a new per-zspage array of objcg pointers to track per-memcg-lruvec memory usage. The changes are expected to improve performance by reducing remote access latency costs and enabling more effective reclaim when pressure is localized to a single cgroup.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about inconsistent naming conventions in the zsmalloc code by renaming zs_object_copy to zs_obj_copy, and also fixed an issue where find_alloced_obj was returning a signed int instead of an unsigned int.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the accuracy of memory consumption accounting in zswap by changing how entries are charged to the zsmalloc layer, specifically using the size_class slot they occupy instead of the compressed object's size.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the inaccuracy of node stats due to the coarse PAGE_SIZE granularity, acknowledged that only NR_ZSWAP_B can be kept accurate, and accepted the small inaccuracy as part of these changes.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing Johannes Weiner's feedback about the placement of obj_cgroup_get() and obj_cgroup_put() calls in the patch series, specifically that the reference count for the pointer should be incremented before setting zspage->objcgs[]. The author agrees to restructure the code to make it more explicit and clear.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 03/11] mm/zsmalloc: Introduce conditional memcg awareness to zs_pool
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces conditional memory cgroup (memcg) awareness to the zsmalloc pool, allowing individual pools to be configured as memcg-aware or not. It adds three new fields to the struct zs_pool: memcg_aware, compressed_stat, and uncompressed_stat, which are used to determine whether memory should be allocated for per-compressed object accounting and what statistics to account towards. The patch also updates the zram driver to create a pool that is not memcg-aware. This change aims to minimize hardcoding specific to zswap in zsmalloc by parametrizing the enum indices for compressed and uncompressed stats.

The reviewer pointed out that storing the memcg stats in every pool is inefficient, as these values are always the same for all zswap pools. They suggested introducing a new struct zs_memcg_params to store these values and passing it from zswap.c while passing NULL from zram.c.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner suggested that the patch should be modified to set pool->memcg_params and use it to gate in zsmalloc.c, indicating a need for additional code changes.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an array of obj_cgroup pointers in the zspage structure to track compressed objects' memory cgroup ownership, if the zs_pool is made memcg-aware at creation time. The error path for alloc_zspage has been simplified by moving it to a jump label. Additionally, the patch includes changes to handle zpdesc allocation and free_zpdesc calls in the error path.

The patch modifies several files to accommodate this change, including drivers/block/zram/zram_drv.c and include/linux/zsmalloc.h.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Redirect zswap_entry->objcg to zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch redirects the zswap layer to use the obj_cgroup pointer stored in the zspage, eliminating duplicate storage and resulting in a net zero memory footprint change. The lifetime and charging of the obj_cgroup is still handled in the zswap layer. Additionally, the patch cleans up the mem_cgroup_from_entry function as it has no remaining callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/11] mm/zsmalloc, zswap: Handle objcg charging and lifetime in zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the zsmalloc layer to handle object cgroup (objcg) charging and lifetime management, specifically for zswap entries. It changes how memory is accounted for in zswap, no longer tracking compressed object size but instead the size of the slot occupied by the entry. This brings memory consumption representation closer to accuracy. Additionally, it removes an unnecessary newline in obj_free and updates related functions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/11] mm/memcontrol: Track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the Linux kernel's memory control system to track MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes rather than PAGE_SIZE units. This change is necessary because Zswap compresses and uncompresses data in PAGE_SIZE units, making it difficult to accurately charge each backing zspage with the correct amount of memory used by a compressed object. By tracking MEMCG_ZSWAPPED in bytes, the kernel can make sub-PAGE_SIZE granularity charging possible for this metric. The patch makes several changes to various kernel files to implement this new tracking mechanism and does not introduce any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/11] mm/vmstat, memcontrol: Track ZSWAP_B, ZSWAPPED_B per-memcg-lruvec
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the Linux kernel to track ZSWAP_B and ZSWAPPED_B statistics per-memcg-lruvec, allowing for more accurate accounting of compressed memory usage across different nodes. The patch moves these statistics from node_stat_item to memcg_stat_item and renames their prefixes accordingly. It also decouples the updates of node stats and memcg-lruvec stats due to the coarse PAGE_SIZE granularity of node stats. As a result, only NR_ZSWAP_B can be kept accurate, while NR_ZSWAPPED_B is a best-effort value that may not accurately account for compressed objects split across nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/11] mm/zsmalloc: Handle single object charge migration in migrate_zspage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch handles single object charge migration in the migrate_zspage function of zsmalloc. It addresses a scenario where compressed objects are migrated from one zspage to another, potentially spanning multiple nodes and pages. The patch calculates how much of each object lives on each page and performs up to 4 uncharge-charges to transfer lruvec and node statistics across affected nodes. This approach avoids enumerating all possible combinations of node migrations, simplifying the process and ensuring accurate accounting of memory usage.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses the issue of charge migration in zpdesc substitution, which can change the lruvec association and node statistics of compressed objects. It introduces a new function `zs_migrate_lruvec` to handle this scenario, taking care to update both the per-lruvec and node-level stats correctly. The function is designed to work around the existing locking mechanism in zsmalloc, which prevents it from calling the usual charge and uncharge functions.

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/mempolicy: track user-defined mempolicy allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds counters to track user-defined memory policy allocations on a per-node basis, providing information about intentional and fallback allocations. The new counters are exposed in /proc/vmstat and nodeN/vmstat files, allowing for easier investigation of pressure on NUMA nodes. The patch addresses the issue that existing numa_* counters cannot be used to determine which user-defined policies drive allocations to a particular node.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds per-policy page allocation counters to track allocations driven by each memory policy (mempolicy) on a NUMA node. The new counters, exposed in /proc/vmstat and memory.numa_stat, follow the existing numa hit/miss/foreign style and provide detailed information about intentional vs fallback allocations. The counters are tracked per-memcg (memory control group) and per-node, allowing for more accurate investigation of pressure on a NUMA node.

Specifically, he pointed out that without step 3 in the original workflow (checking per-policy allocation counters), it is not possible to distinguish whether pages landed on a node intentionally or as a fallback, and that this information can only be obtained by checking /proc/*/numa_maps after following steps 1-2.

Good call. I can instead do:

if (!mem_cgroup_disabled() && root_mem_cgroup) {

> Is n0=3 and N1=10 because we did those allocations according to the

> then things got migrated due to pressure?

That ambiguity should be resolved with this patch.

> 
> Do these counters let you capture that, or does it just make the numbers
> even more meaningless?

You would be able to look at the new counters and see that the
allocations were distributed evenly at the time of allocation. If an
imbalance is observed afterward we would know that it was due to
migration.

> 
> The page allocator will happily fallback to other nodes - even when a
> mempolicy is present - because mempolicy is more of a suggestion rather
> than a rule (unlike cpusets).  So I'd like to understand how these
> counters are intended to be used a little better.

That was the motivation for v2. In the previous rev, there was debate on
the lack of accounting for the fallback cases. So in this patch we
account for the fallbacks by making use of miss/foreign. In terms of how
the counters are intended to be used, the workflow would resemble:

1) Pressure/OOMs reported while system-wide memory is free.
2) Check /proc/zoneinfo or per-node stats in .../nodeN/vmstat to narrow
    down node(s) under pressure.
3) Check per-policy hit/miss/foreign counters (added by this patch) on
    node(s) to see what policy is driving allocations there (intentional
    vs fallback).
4) Use /proc/*/numa_maps to identify tasks using the policy.

>> 1) Pressure/OOMs reported while system-wide memory is free.
>> 2) Check per-node pgscan/pgsteal stats (provided by patch 2) to narrow
>> down node(s) under pressure. They become available in
>> /sys/devices/system/node/nodeN/vmstat.
>> 3) Check per-policy allocation counters (this patch) on that node to
>> find what policy was driving it. Same readout at nodeN/vmstat.
>> 4) Now use /proc/*/numa_maps to identify tasks using the policy.
> 

Good call. I'll add a workflow adapted for the current approach in
the next revision. I included it in another response in this thread, but
I'll repeat here because it will make it easier to answer your question
below.

1) Pressure/OOMs reported while system-wide memory is free.
2) Check /proc/zoneinfo or per-node stats in .../nodeN/vmstat to narrow
    down node(s) under pressure.
3) Check per-policy hit/miss/foreign counters (added by this patch) on
    node(s) to see what policy is driving allocations there (intentional
    vs fallback).
4) Use /proc/*/numa_maps to identify tasks using the policy.

> One question.  If we have to search /proc/*/numa_maps, why can't we
> find all necessary information via /proc/*/numa_maps?  For example,
> which VMA uses the most pages on the node?  Which policy is used in the
> VMA? ...
> 

There's a gap in the flow of information if we go straight from a node
in question to numa_maps. Without step 3 above, we can't distinguish
whether pages landed there intentionally, as a fallback, or were
migrated sometime after the allocation. These new counters track the
results of allocations at the time they happen, preserving that
information regardless of what may happen later on.

numa_maps only show live snapshots with no attribution. Even if we
tracked them over time, there's no way to determine if the allocations
exist as a result of a policy decision.

Tracking other sources of pages on a node (migration, etc) is
beyond the goal of this patch.

How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.

3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.

>>
>> The existing counters don't account for node masks in the policies that
>> make use of them. An allocation can land on a node in the mask and still
>> be considered a miss because it wasn't the preferred node.
> 
> That sounds like we could just a new counter e.g. numa_hit_preferred and
> adjust definitions accordingly? Or some other variant that fills the gap?

It's an interesting thought. Looking into these existing counters more,
the in-kernel direct node allocations, which don't fall under any
mempolicy, are also included in these stats. One good example might be
include/linux/skbuff.h, where __dev_alloc_pages() calls
alloc_pages_node_noprof(NUMA_NO_NODE, ...) which eventually reaches
zone_statistics() and increments the stats. So if we applied the
hit/miss/foreign semantics in this patch to the existing counters we
would be mixing allocations that are in and out of policy, losing the
accuracy.

The new 3 counters I last proposed (in an effort to reduce the amount of
new counters as much as possible) would isolate mempolicy allocs and be
named to reflect that: numa_mpol_{hit,miss,foreign}.

On Fri, Mar 06, 2026 at 08:55:20PM -0800, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
> When investigating pressure on a NUMA node, there is no straightforward way
> to determine which policies are driving allocations to it.

>     - for other policies, allocation succeeded on intended node
>     - counted on the node of the allocation

>     - counted on other node

> Signed-off-by: JP Kobryn (Meta) <jp.kobryn@linux.dev>

[...]

> +
> +	rcu_read_lock();
> +	memcg = mem_cgroup_from_task(current);
> +
> +	if (is_hit) {
> +		lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, NODE_DATA(actual_nid));
> +		mod_lruvec_state(lruvec, hit_idx, nr_pages);
> +	} else {
> +		/* account for miss on the fallback node */
> +		lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, NODE_DATA(actual_nid));
> +		mod_lruvec_state(lruvec, hit_idx + 1, nr_pages);
> +
> +		/* account for foreign on the intended node */
> +		lruvec = mem_cgroup_lruvec(memcg, NODE_DATA(intended_nid));
> +		mod_lruvec_state(lruvec, hit_idx + 2, nr_pages);
> +	}

This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?

---

On Mon, Mar 09, 2026 at 09:17:43PM -0700, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
> On 3/9/26 4:43 PM, Shakeel Butt wrote:
> > On Fri, Mar 06, 2026 at 08:55:20PM -0800, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
[...]
> > 
> > This seems like monotonic increasing metrics and I think you don't care about
> > their absolute value but rather rate of change. Any reason this can not be
> > achieved through tracepoints and BPF combination?
> 
> We have the per-node reclaim stats (pg{steal,scan,refill}) in
> nodeN/vmstat and memory.numa_stat now. The new stats in this patch would
> be collected from the same source. They were meant to be used together,
> so it seemed like a reasonable location. I think the advantage over
> tracepoints is we get the observability on from the start and it would
> be simple to extend existing programs that already read stats from the
> cgroup dir files.

Convenience is not really justifying the cost of adding 18 counters,
particularly in memcg. We can argue about adding just in system level metrics
but not for memcg.

counter_cost = nr_cpus * nr_nodes * nr_memcg * 16 (struct lruvec_stats_percpu)

On a typical prod machine, we can see 1000s of memcg, 100s of cpus and couple of
numa nodes. So, a single counter's cost can range from 200KiB to MiBs. This does
not seem like a cost we should force everyone to pay.

If you really want these per-memcg and assuming these metrics are updated in
non-performance critical path, we can try to decouple these and other reclaim
related stats from rstat infra. That would at least reduce nr_cpus factor in the
above equation to 1. Though we will need to actually evaluate the performance
for the change before committing to it.

> + *     - for other policies, allocation succeeded on intended node
> + *     - counted on the node of the allocation
> + *   miss
> + *     - allocation intended for other node, but happened on this one
> + *     - counted on other node
> + *   foreign

> +		break;
> +	case MPOL_INTERLEAVE:

> +		break;
> +	case MPOL_WEIGHTED_INTERLEAVE:
> +		hit_idx = NUMA_MPOL_WEIGHTED_INTERLEAVE_HIT;
> +		break;
> +	default:
> +		hit_idx = NUMA_MPOL_LOCAL_HIT;
> +		break;
> +	}
> +
> +	if (pol->mode == MPOL_BIND || pol->mode == MPOL_PREFERRED_MANY)
> +		is_hit = node_isset(actual_nid, pol->nodes);

> +	}

On 3/13/26 07:14, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
> On 3/12/26 10:07 PM, Huang, Ying wrote:
>> "JP Kobryn (Meta)" <jp.kobryn@linux.dev> writes:
>> 
>>> On 3/12/26 6:40 AM, Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) wrote:
>>>
>>> How about I change from per-policy hit/miss/foreign triplets to a single
>>> aggregated policy triplet (i.e. just 3 new counters which account for
>>> all policies)? They would follow the same hit/miss/foreign semantics
>>> already proposed (visible in quoted text above). This would still
>>> provide the otherwise missing signal of whether policy-driven
>>> allocations to a node are intentional or fallback.
>>>
>>> Note that I am also planning on moving the stats off of the memcg so the
>>> 3 new counters will be global per-node in response to similar feedback.
>> 
>> Emm, what's the difference between these newly added counters and the
>> existing numa_hit/miss/foreign counters?
> 
> The existing counters don't account for node masks in the policies that
> make use of them. An allocation can land on a node in the mask and still
> be considered a miss because it wasn't the preferred node.

That sounds like we could just a new counter e.g. numa_hit_preferred and
adjust definitions accordingly? Or some other variant that fills the gap?

---

On 3/17/26 07:44, Huang, Ying wrote:
> "JP Kobryn (Meta)" <jp.kobryn@linux.dev> writes:
> 
>>>>
>>>> It's an interesting thought. Looking into these existing counters more,
>>>> the in-kernel direct node allocations, which don't fall under any
>>>> mempolicy, are also included in these stats. One good example might be
>>>> include/linux/skbuff.h, where __dev_alloc_pages() calls
>>>> alloc_pages_node_noprof(NUMA_NO_NODE, ...) which eventually reaches
>>>> zone_statistics() and increments the stats.
>>> IIUC, the default memory policy is used here, that is, MPOL_LOCAL.
>>
>> I'm not seeing that. zone_statistics() is eventually reached.
>> alloc_pages_mpol() is not.
> 
> Yes.  The page isn't allocated through alloc_pages_mpol().  For example,
> if we want to allocate pages for the kernel instead of user space
> applications.  However, IMHO, the equivalent memory policy is
> MPOL_LOCAL, that is, allocate from local node firstly, then fallback to
> other nodes.  I don't think that alloc_pages_mpol() is so special.

Agree, it's equivalent to MPOL_LOCAL.

> ---
> Best Regards,
> Huang, Ying

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch author's response clarifies the distinction between allocations with and without a user-defined memory policy.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (Meta)
review_comments.summary: Raised a question about the distinction between allocations with and without a user-defined memory policy, pointing out that no mempolicy is set in this case.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] mm/mempolicy: track page allocations per mempolicy
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to track page allocations per memory policy, allowing for more detailed statistics and debugging capabilities. The problem it solves is the lack of visibility into how different memory policies are affecting system performance and resource allocation. The approach taken involves modifying the alloc_pages_mpol function to collect and report allocation statistics on a per-mempolicy basis.

review_comments.author: Huang, Ying
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle kernel allocations correctly, as it relies on alloc_pages_mpol(), which is intended for user space applications, and suggested using MPOL_LOCAL instead to allocate from local nodes first
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes.  The page isn't allocated through alloc_pages_mpol().  For example,
if we want to allocate pages for the kernel instead of user space
applications.  However, IMHO, the equivalent memory policy is
MPOL_LOCAL, that is, allocate from local node firstly, then fallback to
other nodes.  I don't think that alloc_pages_mpol() is so special.

---
Best Regards,
Huang, Ying

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that they took the reviewer's statement about default memory policy usage 'literally' and agreed on behavioral equivalence, but emphasized the distinction between allocations with and without a user-defined mempolicy in their v3 patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sure. My response was based on how you said, "the default memory policy
is used here". I took that literally. I agree on the behavioral
equivalence, but the important point is that no mempolicy is set. In the
v3 patch which was recently sent out, I'm using that aspect to
distinguish between allocations with a user-defined mempolicy and
allocations without one.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCHv7.1 12/18] mm/hugetlb: Remove fake head pages
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series optimizes HugeTLB Vmemmap by removing fake head pages, which simplifies compound_head() and maintains memory savings. The new mask-based compound_info encoding allows for true sharing of tail pages without fake heads.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCHv7 09/18] mm/hugetlb: Defer vmemmap population for bootmem hugepages
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch modifies the behavior of hugepage allocation during boot, deferring the population of vmemmap for bootmem hugepages until a later stage.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author reaffirmed their stance on keeping the SECTION_IS_VMEMMAP_PREINIT flag, citing its purpose in core-sparse and handling in hugetlb_vmemmap_init_late(), indicating no planned fix for the reviewer's concern.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think keeping the flag is right thing to do.

SECTION_IS_VMEMMAP_PREINIT indicates to core-sparse that the section
should not be populated and it will be initialized elsewhere. Even in
!hugetlb_bootmem_page_zones_valid() we take care of it in
hugetlb_vmemmap_init_late().

And, as you mentioned, nobody looks at the flag after boot.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 08/22] x86/virt/seamldr: Allocate and populate a module update request
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch corrects an issue in the x86/virt/seamldr module where it incorrectly handles memory alignment for a signature (@sig) that is not 4k aligned. The fix involves adjusting the pointer to the next 4k boundary when handling such cases. Additionally, the patch adds a comment to clarify how block->offset_of_module is calculated relative to the start of the struct tdx_blob.

review_comments.author: Chao Gao
review_comments.summary:
reviewer pointed out that PAGE_SIZE is not guaranteed to be 4096 and suggested two options to address the concern: adding a WARN_ON_ONCE or handling it as in the code above

Reviewer pointed out that the code assumes @sig is page-aligned, but it's actually 4k aligned in VA; they suggested adding a comment to clarify that block->offset_of_module is relative to the start of struct tdx_blob and not blob->data
review_comments.raw_body:
Dan's concern was that PAGE_SIZE is not guaranteed to be 4096.

I agree that PAGE_SIZE is 4K on x86 today. But to address that concern, I saw
two options:

  1. Add WARN_ON_ONCE(PAGE_SIZE != SZ_4K), or

I didn't find existing code using option 1 in x86, so I chose option 2.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 3/4] x86/virt/tdx: Add SEAMCALL wrapper for TDH.SYS.DISABLE
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a SEAMCALL wrapper for TDH.SYS.DISABLE, allowing the kernel to disable TDX (Trusted Domain eXtensions) system calls in a more controlled manner. The change addresses issues with delayed errors and debugging difficulties when dealing with stale TDX modules.

review_comments.author: Edgecombe, P
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that TDH.SYS.DISABLE blocks other seamcalls, causing SEAMCALL failures when TDX is disabled while running TDX-related code, and suggested adding a big block comment to explain the TDX enable/disable lifecycle quirks.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yea, a side effect of TDH.SYS.DISABLE is that it blocks other seamcalls while it
is executing. I guess the scenario here is TDX init racing with kexec.

But in general if TDX is disabled while any TDX stuff is running, the seamcalls
will be surprised. This is not fully related to TDH.SYS.DISABLE, because VMXOFF
will also cause similar SEAMCALL failures. Each SEAMCALL path would need to
handle the rug pull. And probably we need to balance harmless noise against the
code it takes to be quieter.

try_init_module_global() is different in that it's kernel side code that gets
confused, but I'm not sure how it could be handled in a non-racy way either.
So... I'd think to leave it. Maybe what we really need is a big block comment
about TDX enable/disable lifecycle quirks.
